clinical-path-foundations.crossDomainJustification: This module is intentionally a CP orientation layer spanning the major exam roots before learners branch into deeper single-domain modules.
clinical-path-foundations.boardMasteryFocusTitle: Foundational CP workflow: specimen, result, escalation, and next-step logic
mustKnowConcepts: CP board performance is driven by operational interpretation rather than isolated fact recall.
Specimen quality, result interpretation, and safety escalation are cross-cutting skills across CP roots.
The learner should know when to stay broad and when to route into a deeper domain-specific workup.
mustNotMissPitfalls: Treating CP as disconnected silos without shared consult logic.
Missing the distinction between routine interpretation and critical-result escalation.
Choosing an advanced subspecialty step before the core lab-medicine framing is stable.
classicBoardStemPatterns: A mixed CP stem where the key task is selecting the next best laboratory action.
A result interpretation problem that is unsafe without escalation.
A broad CP consult scenario requiring domain triage before disease naming.
calculationOrInterpretationTasks: Choose which CP domain owns the next step in a mixed stem.
Interpret whether the first move is validation, reflex testing, or urgent communication.
Explain the report-level consequence of a CP result.
commonWrongAnswerTraps: Jumping to a specific disease label before establishing the operational frame.
Ignoring safety or quality cues in a cross-domain CP vignette.
Assuming every CP question is primarily about memorized disease taxonomy.

Hematopathology > Non-Neoplastic Disorders of Erythrocytes > PNH
hematology-red-cell-core.boardMasteryFocusTitle: Anemia, hemolysis, marrow-linked red-cell reasoning, and escalation cues
mustKnowConcepts: Peripheral blood, marrow, and specialized testing each answer different anemia questions.
Immune, non-immune, and microangiopathic hemolysis must be separated quickly.
PNH and hemoglobinopathy stems are often interpretation-plus-next-test questions.
mustNotMissPitfalls: Calling hemolysis without integrating smear and laboratory context.
Missing when marrow evaluation changes the differential.
Overlooking high-risk hemolysis patterns that require urgent escalation.
classicBoardStemPatterns: Anemia with smear abnormalities and targeted next-study selection.
Dark urine, cytopenias, or thrombosis suggesting PNH reasoning.
Hemoglobinopathy stem requiring distinction among common and less common variants.
calculationOrInterpretationTasks: Interpret whether the pattern is immune, non-immune, or microangiopathic hemolysis.
Choose when marrow evaluation is necessary.
State the next test that best resolves the red-cell differential.
commonWrongAnswerTraps: Choosing a confirmatory test that does not match the red-cell mechanism.
Ignoring marrow context in persistent or multilineage abnormalities.
Anchoring on one anemia label before the pattern is fully classified.

Hematopathology > Hemostasis and Thrombosis > Platelet Disorders and von Willebrand Disease > Thrombocytopenia > Immune
Hematopathology > Hemostasis and Thrombosis > Platelet Disorders and von Willebrand Disease > von Willebrand Disease
Hematopathology > Hemostasis and Thrombosis > Thrombophilic Disorders > Laboratory Diagnosis of Thrombosis and Thrombophilia
coagulation-hemostasis-core.boardMasteryFocusTitle: Bleeding, clotting, inhibitor, and anticoagulant-monitoring interpretation
mustKnowConcepts: PT, aPTT, platelet issues, fibrinolysis, and thrombophilia belong to different decision branches.
Mixing studies and follow-up testing are next-step interpretation tasks, not rote lists.
DIC, TTP/HUS, HIT, and major bleeding stems are safety-driven CP problems.
mustNotMissPitfalls: Ordering broad panels before interpreting the first-line coagulation pattern.
Missing when a coagulation result implies urgent communication.
classicBoardStemPatterns: Isolated prolonged aPTT or PT with next-test logic.
Thrombocytopenia with clotting or hemolysis concern.
Anticoagulant or antiplatelet monitoring stem requiring method-aware interpretation.
calculationOrInterpretationTasks: Interpret first-line coagulation results into the correct branch of workup.
Choose the next most informative follow-up test.
Identify when the result requires immediate escalation.
commonWrongAnswerTraps: Treating mixing studies as final diagnoses instead of branch points.
Missing the difference between inherited and acquired inhibitor logic.
Using anticoagulant-monitoring rules in the wrong assay context.

transfusion-cellular-therapy-core.crossDomainJustification: This module is anchored in blood banking and transfusion medicine, but it intentionally includes HLA-linked support and cellular-therapy decision points that cross into adjacent CP governance.
transfusion-cellular-therapy-core.boardMasteryFocusTitle: Compatibility, reaction workup, platelet support, HLA, and cellular-therapy decision logic
mustKnowConcepts: Blood bank stems are usually about safety, interpretation, and the next laboratory action.
Compatibility testing, hemolysis workup, HLA-linked issues, and product selection are connected but distinct branches.
The correct answer is often the safest operational step before the most detailed immunohematology explanation.
mustNotMissPitfalls: Continuing a transfusion problem-solving sequence before stopping the transfusion and escalating.
Confusing product-selection questions with antibody-identification questions.
Ignoring HLA or platelet refractoriness context when it changes support strategy.
classicBoardStemPatterns: Acute transfusion reaction with clerical check and DAT-linked workup.
Crossmatch or antibody-screen discrepancy requiring next-step selection.
Platelet or HLA support question asking for the safest product strategy.
calculationOrInterpretationTasks: Interpret the immediate significance of a transfusion reaction pattern.
Choose the next blood-bank test or product decision.
State when regulatory or safety communication is required.
commonWrongAnswerTraps: Choosing advanced serology before core safety steps.
Mixing HLA support logic with routine red-cell compatibility logic.
Underestimating how often the exam tests escalation rather than final etiology.

abpathAnchorSet: Microbiology > Bacteriology
Microbiology > Mycology
Microbiology > Virology
Microbiology > Parasitology
clinical-microbiology-core.nearestValidReason: workflow concept distributed across multiple spec nodes
clinical-microbiology-core.boardMasteryFocusTitle: Specimen-to-bench reasoning, organism workup, and AST-guided reporting
mustKnowConcepts: Microbiology boards logic is specimen quality plus organism workup plus reporting consequence.
Bench methods and AST belong inside organism-context reasoning.
Critical organism notification and infection-control consequences are part of exam-level interpretation.
mustNotMissPitfalls: Reporting susceptibility without organism or clinical context.
Ignoring when a microbiology finding changes isolation or escalation.
classicBoardStemPatterns: Recent procedure or hospital exposure with resistant organism workup.
Method-selection stem contrasting culture, antigen, or molecular approaches.
calculationOrInterpretationTasks: Interpret whether the microbiology pattern supports immediate reporting, reflex testing, or recollection.
Choose the next identification or susceptibility step.
State the practical consequence of the result.
commonWrongAnswerTraps: Choosing a definitive test that bypasses specimen quality logic.
Treating AST as detached from stewardship and communication.
Using a narrow microbiology method outside its proper interpretive scope.

abpathAnchorSet: Chemical Pathology > Specimen Collection and Processing
Chemical Pathology > Method Validation & QC
Chemical Pathology > Method Validation & QC > Analytical Measurement Range (AMR)
Chemical Pathology > Method Validation & QC > Carryover
Chemical Pathology > Method Validation & QC > Coefficient of Variation (CV)
Chemical Pathology > Method Validation & QC > Linearity
chemical-pathology-core.nearestValidReason: workflow concept distributed across multiple spec nodes
chemical-pathology-core.boardMasteryFocusTitle: Clinical biochemistry interpretation, endocrine-metabolic patterns, and method-aware CP reasoning
mustKnowConcepts: Chemistry stems are often about interpreting the pattern plus recognizing method limits.
QC, AMR, linearity, and carryover are testable operational concepts, not only laboratory admin details.
Endocrine and metabolic teaching labels should map back to chemistry interpretation rather than masquerade as literal spec nodes.
mustNotMissPitfalls: Ignoring assay or specimen limitations when the value looks clinically dramatic.
Treating endocrine names as sufficient without biochemical interpretation.
Missing when a result pattern is primarily a QC or interference problem.
classicBoardStemPatterns: Unexpected chemistry result requiring distinction between true pathology and method issue.
Endocrine or metabolic profile asking for the next best confirmatory test.
QC or validation stem requiring recognition of the failing parameter.
calculationOrInterpretationTasks: Interpret whether the chemistry pattern is analytically trustworthy.
Choose the next confirmatory or alternate method when needed.
Apply QC or validation logic to a practical lab scenario.
commonWrongAnswerTraps: Treating an interference problem as a disease problem.
Choosing the most specific disease label instead of the most defensible chemistry interpretation.
Ignoring the operational meaning of validation or QC terminology.
